1: apostolos Christou Iēsou kat' epitagēn theou — 'apostle of Christ Jesus by command (epitagē) of God.' Epitagē (command, authoritative order) is stronger than thelēma (will) used in Paul's earlier letters (1 Cor 1:1; 2 Cor 1:1; Eph 1:1). The shift to 'command' language signals the Pastoral Epistles' heightened concern with apostolic authority and the legitimacy of the tradition Paul is transmitting. Sōtēros hēmōn theou (God our Savior) — the title 'Savior' applied to God is characteristically Pastoral (1 Tim 2:3; 4:10; Titus 1:3; 2:10; 3:4) and reflects the LXX's regular use of sōtēr for YHWH as deliverer. Christ Jesus our hope: elpis is personalized — not a thing hoped for but a person.
2: gnēsiō teknō en pistei — 'true son (gnēsios — legitimate, genuine, not illegitimate) in the faith.' The word distinguishes Timothy from false teachers who claim apostolic authority without genuine relationship or commission. The triple benediction 'grace, mercy, peace' (charis, eleos, eirēnē) expands the standard Pauline opening 'grace and peace' with eleos (mercy) — the distinctively OT covenantal term for YHWH's steadfast loyalty (hesed). Its addition in the Pastoral salutations (also 2 Tim 1:2; Titus 1:4 lacks it) may signal the heightened vulnerability of Timothy's situation: he needs mercy as well as grace.
3: heterodidaskalein — 'teach differently' (heteros = different in kind, not allos = different in number). The compound is a Pauline coinage (appearing again in 6:3) marking the false teachers' problem as categorical difference from the apostolic norm, not merely minor variation. The false teaching is not identified by name but characterized functionally: it promotes muthois (myths) and genealogiais (genealogies). 'Myths and genealogies' likely refers to Jewish speculative interpretation involving OT genealogies developed in Second Temple literature — elaborations beyond what the text says that generate controversy rather than edification (oikodomia).
4: muthois kai genealogiais aperantois — 'myths and endless genealogies.' Aperantois (endless, boundless) describes content that generates only ekzētēseis (controversies, investigations) rather than oikonomian theou (God's stewardship — some MSS read oikodomēn, edification). The contrast is between speculation that multiplies questions and the gospel that builds up. The participial parechousin (promote, furnish) makes the myths and genealogies themselves the agents of the harm — the content is structurally corrosive, not merely peripheral.
5: to de telos tēs paraggeleias estin agapē — 'the goal (telos) of the command is love.' Telos is end/goal in the teleological sense — love is not incidental to the instruction but its intended terminus. The love described is then triple-sourced: from a katharas kardias (clean heart — the OT moral center, Ps 51:10), an agathēs suneidēseōs (good conscience — the Hellenistic-Jewish moral self-awareness Paul develops throughout the Pastorals), and an anupokritou pisteōs (sincere/unhypocritical faith). The three-source structure answers the false teachers' problem: they have neither clean hearts nor good consciences nor unhypocritical faith, which is why their teaching produces controversy rather than love.
6: hōn tines astochēsantes — 'which some having missed/deviated from.' Astocheō (to miss the mark, deviate from the target) is a Pastoral neologism (also 1 Tim 6:21; 2 Tim 2:18). The image is of an archer or a person on a path who fails to hit or follow the right course. The deviation is from the triple-source of love (v. 5), not from doctrine directly — the false teachers' error is moral before it is doctrinal. They 'turned' (exetrapēsan — aorist passive of ektrepō, to turn aside) to mataiologia (meaningless talk, empty discourse) — a term from the LXX applied to idol-worship.
7: thelontes einai nomodidaskaloi mē noountes — 'wanting to be teachers of the law, not understanding.' The aorist participle noountes (understanding/comprehending) is negated: they lack the understanding that would qualify them for what they desire. Nomodidaskalos (law-teacher) appears in Luke 5:17 and Acts 5:34 (Gamaliel) for recognized Jewish scribal teachers. The false teachers want this honorable title without the knowledge it requires. The emphatic mē noountes mēte ha legousin mēte peri tinōn diabebaiountai — 'not understanding either what they are saying or what things they confidently assert' — piles up the negations: double ignorance about both content and reference.
8: oida de hoti kalos ho nomos ean tis autō nomimōs chrētai — 'we know that the law is good if one uses it lawfully/legitimately.' The adverb nomimōs (according to law, lawfully) is a rhetorical paradox: the law must be used law-fully. The principle sets up the next verses: the law functions differently for different categories of people. For the righteous, it is not given (ou keitai — not laid down); for the unrighteous, it is the diagnostic instrument that reveals and names their sin.
9: oti dikaiō nomos ou keitai — 'that law is not laid down for a just person.' The dikaios (righteous/just) person does not need the law as an external coercive boundary — they live its requirement by nature of their character transformation. The catalogue of sinners that follows (vv. 9-10) is structured in roughly legal/Decalogal order: the first three pairs (lawless and rebellious / ungodly and sinful / unholy and profane) echo the first table of the Decalog (duties toward God); the subsequent pairs (patricides/matricides, murderers, sexually immoral, homosexuals, slave-traders, liars) echo the second table (duties toward neighbor). The catalogue diagnoses society as needing law precisely because it is lawless.
10: arsenokoitais — 'those who practice homosexuality' (ESV); 'men who have sex with men' (NIV); 'sodomites' (NRSV). The term is a compound of arsēn (male) + koitē (bed/lying) and appears first in the NT (here and 1 Cor 6:9) and in scattered later Greek sources. It may be a coinage based on the LXX of Lev 18:22 and 20:13 (arsenos koitēn). The semantic range is debated: some limit it to male prostitution or pederasty; others read it as a broad term for same-sex sexual activity between males. The parallel pornois (sexually immoral) suggests a broader sexual ethics category. The phrase 'and whatever else is contrary to sound doctrine' (hygiainousē didaskalia) — healthy/health-producing teaching — frames all the catalogue entries as violations of the doctrinal health-standard.
11: to euangelion tēs doxēs tou makariou theou — 'the gospel of the glory of the blessed God.' The genitive chain: gospel ← of the glory ← of the blessed God. The gospel is characterized by glory (doxa) — not merely information but a disclosure of divine splendor. Makarios (blessed/happy) applied to God is unusual in the NT (only here and 6:15); it derives from the LXX and Hellenistic usage. Paul's addition 'which was entrusted to me' — the perfect passive episte(u)thēn — marks the gospel as a trust held in fiduciary relationship: Paul does not own it but stewards it.
12: charin echō tō endunamōsanti me Christō Iēsou tō kyriō hēmōn — 'I give thanks to Christ Jesus our Lord who strengthened me.' The aorist participle endunamōsanti (having empowered/strengthened) points to Christ's past act of equipping Paul for ministry. The triple designation — 'Christ Jesus our Lord' — is solemn. Pistos me hēgēsato (he considered me faithful/trustworthy) — the aorist indicates a past divine assessment. God's assessment of trustworthiness precedes and creates the appointment: Paul did not become trustworthy before being appointed; the appointment itself was the act of divine grace that created the trustworthiness.
13: to proteron onta blasphēmon kai diōktēn kai hubristēn — 'formerly being a blasphemer and a persecutor and an insolent man.' The three terms escalate: blasphēmos (blasphemer) — speaking against God and Christ; diōktēs (persecutor) — hunting down the church; hubristēs (violent aggressor, insolent one) — the term covers both arrogance and its violent expression. The softening — ēleēthēn hoti agnoōn epoiēsa en apistiā (I was shown mercy because I acted ignorantly in unbelief) — distinguishes Paul's sin from the willful apostasy of 6:20-21: ignorance-based sin is in a different category than deliberate rejection of known truth (cf. Num 15:30 on the distinction between inadvertent and high-handed sin).
14: hyperepleonasen de hē charis tou kyriou hēmōn — 'the grace of our Lord overflowed beyond measure.' The compound hyperepleonasen (super-abounded/overflowed) is a Pauline coinage (hapax in NT) that intensifies the abundance already expressed in perisseuō. Grace did not merely appear but exceeded all expectation of measure — it overflowed the container of Paul's sin. The accompanying gifts — faith and love en Christō Iēsou — are not Paul's own virtues but qualities that exist in Christ and flow to him in union with Christ: the faith and love of the Christian are participatory, not self-generated.
15: pistos ho logos kai pasēs apodochēs axios — 'trustworthy is the saying and worthy of full acceptance.' The formula pistos ho logos introduces five Pastoral Epistle sayings (1 Tim 1:15; 3:1; 4:9; 2 Tim 2:11; Titus 3:8), marking each as a recognized and authoritative statement within the community — perhaps early creedal or catechetical formulations. The saying here: Christos Iēsous ēlthen eis ton kosmon hamartōlous sōsai — Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners. Ēlthen eis ton kosmon (came into the world) implies pre-existence and mission: Christ entered a world he was not originating from. The present-tense self-identification hōn prōtos eimi egō (of whom I am the foremost/first) — Paul does not say 'I was the foremost' but present tense: his continued awareness of the depth of his sin is the ground of ongoing marvel at grace.
16: alla dia touto ēleēthēn hina en emoi prōtō endeixētai Christos Iēsous tēn hapasan makrothumian — 'for this reason I received mercy, so that in me, as the foremost, Christ Jesus might display his complete patience.' Endeixētai (might display/demonstrate) — the aorist subjunctive of purpose: Paul's conversion was not merely personal salvation but a public demonstration for future generations. Makrothumia (patience, long-suffering, slow to anger) is the word that in the LXX describes YHWH's restraint of judgment (Exod 34:6; Num 14:18). Christ's 'complete' (hapasan) patience displayed in Paul becomes the exemplar (hypotypōsin — pattern, prototype) for all future believers.
17: tō de basilei tōn aiōnōn aphtartō aoratō monō theō timē kai doxa eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn — 'to the King of the ages, immortal, invisible, the only God.' The doxology breaks from the personal testimony with a liturgical outburst. Basileus tōn aiōnōn (King of the ages/eternity) — the genitive may be temporal (reigning over the ages) or qualitative (the eternal King). Aphtartos (incorruptible, immortal) and aoratos (invisible) apply to God the qualities that Paul elsewhere ascribes to resurrection bodies and to God's eternal nature (Rom 1:20). The doxology's form — timē kai doxa eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn — is the standard Jewish synagogue closing blessing adapted to Christological worship.
18: tautēn tēn paraggellian paratithemai soi teknon Timothee — 'this command I entrust to you, child Timothy.' Paratithemai (to place beside, entrust) is the fiduciary verb of depositing a trust: the command is a parathēkē (deposit) that Timothy must guard (cf. 6:20's tēn parathēkēn phulaxon). The phrase kata tas proagousas epi se prophēteias (in accordance with the prophecies previously made about you) refers to prophetic words that accompanied Timothy's commissioning — possibly at his ordination (4:14). Paul grounds the command in divine authorization: the commission Timothy received is not Paul's opinion but follows a prophetic pattern already spoken over him.
19: echōn pistin kai agathēn suneidēsin hēn tines apōsamenoi — 'holding faith and a good conscience, which some have rejected.' Apōsamenoi (having thrust away, rejected) — the aorist middle of apōtheō, used of deliberate, forceful rejection. The rejection was not passive drift but active refusal of conscience. The result: peri tēn pistin enauagēsan (they have shipwrecked with regard to the faith). The shipwreck metaphor is vivid — not a gentle drifting off course but a catastrophic structural failure. Conscience and faith are linked: when conscience is suppressed, faith does not survive independently.
20: hous paredōka tō Satana hina paideuthosin mē blasphēmein — 'whom I have handed over to Satan so that they may be taught not to blaspheme.' The disciplinary formula echoes 1 Cor 5:5 (the immoral man handed to Satan for destruction of the flesh). The paradox: Satan, the adversary, becomes the instrument of corrective discipline. The goal is remedial (paideuthosin — the verb of parental discipline and education, used in Heb 12:6-11 for divine chastening) not punitive: the outcome sought is that they 'learn not to blaspheme.' The naming of Hymenaeus and Alexander (real, traceable individuals) marks this as public ecclesial discipline, not private pastoral concern.
